title: Art Notions in the Age of (Mis)anthropic AI
summary: In this paper, I take the cultural effects of generative artificial intelligence (generative AI) as a context for examining a broader perspective of AI's impact on contemporary art notions. After the introductory overview of generative AI, I summarize the distinct but often confused aspects of art notions and review the principal lines in which AI influences them: the strategic normalization of AI through art, the representation of AI art in the artworld, academia, and AI research, and the mutual permeability of art and kitsch in the digital culture. I connect these notional factors with the conceptual and ideological substrate of the computer science and AI industry, which blends the machinic agency fetishism, the equalization of computers and humans, the sociotechnical blindness, and cyberlibertarianism. The overtones of alienation, sociopathy, and misanthropy in the disparate but somehow coalescing philosophical premises, technical ideas, and political views in this substrate remain underexposed in AI studies so, in the closing discussion, I outline their manifestations in generative AI and introduce several viewpoints for a further critique of AI's cultural zeitgeist. They add a touch of skepticism to pondering how technological trends change our understanding of art and in which directions they stir its social, economic, and political roles.

processed.summary:
- Analyzes the **cultural zeitgeist** and ideological substrate of **Generative AI**, focusing on the strategic normalization of AI through contemporary art notions.
- Investigates the impact of **machinic agency fetishism** and the equalization of humans and computers on the development and social perception of AI models.
- Critiques the **sociotechnical blindness** within the AI industry, highlighting how misanthropic philosophical premises influence the current direction of generative technologies.
key_results: Identification of 'strategic normalization' as a tactic for integrating AI into high-culture art sectors.
Analysis of the blurred boundaries between high art and digital kitsch driven by generative outputs.
Linking of AI industry ideologies to machinic agency fetishism and cyberlibertarianism.
Documentation of the 'misanthropic' overtones present in the philosophical foundations of AI development.
Proposing a critical framework for assessing AI's shifting social, economic, and political roles.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv research critiques how the AI industry utilizes art to normalize misanthropic ideologies and machinic agency fetishism.

title: SOMtime the World Ain$'$t Fair: Violating Fairness Using Self-Organizing Maps
summary: Unsupervised representations are widely assumed to be neutral with respect to sensitive attributes when those attributes are withheld from training. We show that this assumption is false. Using SOMtime, a topology-preserving representation method based on high-capacity Self-Organizing Maps, we demonstrate that sensitive attributes such as age and income emerge as dominant latent axes in purely unsupervised embeddings, even when explicitly excluded from the input. On two large-scale real-world datasets (the World Values Survey across five countries and the Census-Income dataset), SOMtime recovers monotonic orderings aligned with withheld sensitive attributes, achieving Spearman correlations of up to 0.85, whereas PCA and UMAP typically remain below 0.23 (with a single exception reaching 0.31), and against t-SNE and autoencoders which achieve at most 0.34. Furthermore, unsupervised segmentation of SOMtime embeddings produces demographically skewed clusters, demonstrating downstream fairness risks without any supervised task. These findings establish that \textit{fairness through unawareness} fails at the representation level for ordinal sensitive attributes and that fairness auditing must extend to unsupervised components of machine learning pipelines. We have made the code available at~ https://github.com/JosephBingham/SOMtime

• Proposes **SOMtime**, a high-capacity **Self-Organizing Map (SOM)** architecture designed for topology-preserving unsupervised representation learning.
• Challenges the **'fairness through unawareness'** assumption by demonstrating that sensitive ordinal attributes (e.g., age, income) emerge as dominant latent axes even when excluded from training.
• Benchmarks against **PCA, UMAP, and Autoencoders**, showing SOMtime achieves significantly higher Spearman correlations (0.85) with withheld sensitive features compared to traditional methods (<0.34).
• Argues that **fairness auditing** must be integrated into the unsupervised components of machine learning pipelines to prevent demographically skewed downstream segmentation.
key_results: SOMtime achieved Spearman correlations of up to 0.85 with withheld sensitive attributes like age and income.
Traditional dimensionality reduction methods like PCA and UMAP typically stayed below 0.23 correlation.
State-of-the-art autoencoders and t-SNE reached a maximum correlation of only 0.34, significantly trailing SOMtime.
Unsupervised clustering of SOMtime embeddings resulted in demographically skewed segments without any supervised labels.
Results were consistent across two large-scale datasets: the World Values Survey and the Census-Income dataset.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Joseph Bingham demonstrates that high-capacity SOMtime embeddings recover withheld sensitive attributes, proving fairness through unawareness is technically insufficient.

title: BLM-Guard: Explainable Multimodal Ad Moderation with Chain-of-Thought and Policy-Aligned Rewards
summary: Short-video platforms now host vast multimodal ads whose deceptive visuals, speech and subtitles demand finer-grained, policy-driven moderation than community safety filters. We present BLM-Guard, a content-audit framework for commercial ads that fuses Chain-of-Thought reasoning with rule-based policy principles and a critic-guided reward. A rule-driven ICoT data-synthesis pipeline jump-starts training by generating structured scene descriptions, reasoning chains and labels, cutting annotation costs. Reinforcement learning then refines the model using a composite reward balancing causal coherence with policy adherence. A multitask architecture models intra-modal manipulations (e.g., exaggerated imagery) and cross-modal mismatches (e.g., subtitle-speech drift), boosting robustness. Experiments on real short-video ads show BLM-Guard surpasses strong baselines in accuracy, consistency and generalization.

processed.summary:
- **BLM-Guard** introduces a multimodal content-audit framework that integrates **Chain-of-Thought (CoT)** reasoning with rule-based policy principles for explainable ad moderation.
- The system features an **ICoT data-synthesis pipeline**, leveraging rule-driven instruction to generate structured scene descriptions and labels, significantly lowering manual annotation costs.
- A **multitask architecture** is employed to detect both intra-modal manipulations and cross-modal inconsistencies, such as mismatches between speech and subtitles.
- The framework utilizes **Reinforcement Learning** with a critic-guided composite reward to ensure the model maintains **causal coherence** and strict alignment with moderation policies.
key_results: Outperforms existing safety filters and strong baselines in moderation accuracy.
Improves explainability through structured reasoning chains for every audit decision.
Demonstrates robust generalization across diverse short-video advertising categories.
Successfully identifies subtle deceptive practices like exaggerated imagery and audio-text drift.
Reduces the human-in-the-loop requirement via automated, policy-aligned data generation.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce BLM-Guard, utilizing Chain-of-Thought reasoning and reinforcement learning for explainable, policy-driven multimodal ad moderation.

title: A geometric physics-informed machine learning inference for the neutron star maximum mass and the inverse problem
summary: The existence of a distinct mass boundary between the heaviest neutron stars and the lightest black holes remains in question. It is an artefact of our ignorance of the properties of matter at supra-nuclear densities, which exist in the cores of neutron stars. The study addresses these problems with a physics-informed machine learning approach, guided by astrophysical observations. The Transformer model is trained on an agnostically generated ensemble of equations of state. Two geometric parameters are defined on the mass-radius sequence of a neutron star--the front bending and the back bending. The transformer provides a two-step solution: first, the model predicts the maximum mass and radius using the bending parameters. Second, it predicts the square of the sound speed profile, completing the inverse mapping. The prediction is that massive neutron stars form when the sound speed peaks at low density, leading to strong back-bending and an early phase transition to quark matter. Massive stars favour a stiff equation of state at low density, and the density of matter at the star's core is sufficiently small. The maximum mass for a neutron star predicted by the astrophysical constrained transformer model is $2.477$ solar masses, and a minimum radius of about $11.498$ km for a neutron star of $1.4$ solar masses.

processed.summary:
• Employs a **Transformer-based architecture** trained on agnostically generated ensembles of equations of state to model the relationship between astrophysical data and nuclear matter properties.
• Utilizes **Physics-Informed Machine Learning (PIML)** to define geometric parameters—front and back bending—within mass-radius sequences for more accurate inference.
• Implements a **two-step predictive pipeline** that first calculates maximum mass/radius and subsequently solves the **inverse problem** to map the sound speed profile of dense matter.
• Demonstrates how **Generative AI** techniques can bridge the gap in our understanding of supra-nuclear densities and phase transitions to quark matter.
key_results: Predicted a maximum neutron star mass of 2.477 solar masses.
Calculated a minimum radius of 11.498 km for a standard 1.4 solar mass neutron star.
Found that massive stars are supported by a stiff equation of state at low density.
Identified that sound speed peaks at low density facilitate early phase transitions to quark matter.
Established a correlation between strong 'back-bending' geometric sequences and increased stellar mass.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers use Transformer models and geometric physics-informed ML to solve the inverse problem of neutron star composition.

title: Role and Identity Work of Software Engineering Professionals in the Generative AI Era
summary: The adoption of Generative AI (GenAI) suggests major changes for software engineering, including technical aspects but also human aspects of the professionals involved. One of these aspects is how individuals perceive themselves regarding their work, i.e., their work identity, and the processes they perform to form, adapt and reject these identities, i.e., identity work. Existent studies provide evidence of such identity work of software professionals triggered by the adoption of GenAI, however they do not consider differences among diverse roles, such as developers and testers. In this paper, we argue the need for considering the role as a factor defining the identity work of software professionals. To support our claim, we review some studies regarding different roles and also recent studies on how to adopt GenAI in software engineering. Then, we propose a research agenda to better understand how the role influences identity work of software professionals triggered by the adoption of GenAI, and, based on that, to propose new artifacts to support this adoption. We also discuss the potential implications for practice of the results to be obtained.

processed.summary:
- The paper explores the concept of **identity work** within software engineering, examining how **Generative AI (GenAI)** adoption disrupts how professionals perceive their roles.
- It identifies a critical research gap where the **role-specific impact** (e.g., developers vs. testers) of AI tools on professional identity has been largely overlooked.
- The authors propose a structured **research agenda** to analyze the socio-technical shift from active production to **AI-assisted oversight**.
- The study aims to inform the creation of **new artifacts and frameworks** that support the psychological and professional transition of software workers.
key_results: Identifies that GenAI adoption triggers a process of forming, adapting, or rejecting professional identities.
Argues that a professional's specific role is a primary factor in how GenAI tools are integrated into workflows.
Highlights significant differences in AI perception between software developers and quality assurance (QA) professionals.
Proposes a multi-stage research plan to develop role-based support mechanisms for AI adoption.
Discusses the long-term impact of automated code generation on the perceived value of manual engineering expertise.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers propose a research agenda to investigate how GenAI reshapes the professional identities of developers and testers.

title: Capabilities Ain't All You Need: Measuring Propensities in AI
summary: AI evaluation has primarily focused on measuring capabilities, with formal approaches inspired from Item Response Theory (IRT) being increasingly applied. Yet propensities - the tendencies of models to exhibit particular behaviours - play a central role in determining both performance and safety outcomes. However, traditional IRT describes a model's success on a task as a monotonic function of model capabilities and task demands, an approach unsuited to propensities, where both excess and deficiency can be problematic. Here, we introduce the first formal framework for measuring AI propensities by using a bilogistic formulation for model success, which attributes high success probability when the model's propensity is within an "ideal band". Further, we estimate the limits of the ideal band using LLMs equipped with newly developed task-agnostic rubrics. Applying our framework to six families of LLM models whose propensities are incited in either direction, we find that we can measure how much the propensity is shifted and what effect this has on the tasks. Critically, propensities estimated using one benchmark successfully predict behaviour on held-out tasks. Moreover, we obtain stronger predictive power when combining propensities and capabilities than either separately. More broadly, our framework showcases how rigorous propensity measurements can be conducted and how it yields gains over solely using capability evaluations to predict AI behaviour.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a novel evaluation framework based on **bilogistic Item Response Theory (IRT)** to measure model **propensities**, or the behavioral tendencies of LLMs.
- Moves beyond monotonic capability metrics to define an **ideal band** of behavior where both excess and deficiency in a trait (e.g., verbosity or caution) can degrade performance.
- Utilizes **task-agnostic rubrics** and LLM-based estimation to quantify the limits of optimal model behavior across diverse benchmarks.
- Validates the framework across **six LLM families**, demonstrating that propensity-plus-capability modeling yields significantly higher predictive power for future model behavior.
key_results: Propensities function as non-monotonic determinants of success, where 'too much' of a behavior is often as detrimental as 'too little'.
Measuring propensities allows for the quantification of behavior shifts across different model versions or fine-tuning stages.
Propensity estimates derived from a single benchmark successfully generalize to predict model performance on held-out, unrelated tasks.
The combination of capability and propensity metrics provides a more robust characterization of AI systems than capability alone.
Automated rubrics effectively define the 'ideal band' of behavior, reducing the need for manual per-task evaluation design.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Google DeepMind researchers introduce a bilogistic framework to measure behavioral propensities, improving AI performance prediction over capability-only metrics.

title: SeedFlood: A Step Toward Scalable Decentralized Training of LLMs
summary: This work presents a new approach to decentralized training-SeedFlood-designed to scale for large models across complex network topologies and achieve global consensus with minimal communication overhead. Traditional gossip-based methods suffer from message communication costs that grow with model size, while information decay over network hops renders global consensus inefficient. SeedFlood departs from these practices by exploiting the seed-reconstructible structure of zeroth-order updates and effectively making the messages near-zero in size, allowing them to be flooded to every client in the network. This mechanism makes communication overhead negligible and independent of model size, removing the primary scalability bottleneck in decentralized training. Consequently, SeedFlood enables training in regimes previously considered impractical, such as billion-parameter models distributed across hundreds of clients. Our experiments on decentralized LLM fine-tuning demonstrate thatSeedFlood consistently outperforms gossip-based baselines in both generalization performance and communication efficiency, and even achieves results comparable to first-order methods in large scale settings.

processed.summary:
* **SeedFlood** introduces a decentralized training architecture that utilizes **zeroth-order optimization** to transmit model updates as small, reconstructible random seeds.
* The framework employs a **flooding mechanism** to achieve global consensus across complex network topologies while maintaining near-zero communication overhead.
* By decoupling communication costs from model size, the method enables the training of **billion-parameter models** across hundreds of distributed clients.
* The approach effectively mitigates **information decay** common in gossip-based methods, achieving performance comparable to first-order methods in large-scale settings.
key_results: Achieved communication overhead that is near-zero and independent of model parameter count.
Demonstrated successful decentralized fine-tuning of billion-parameter LLMs across hundreds of nodes.
Outperformed traditional gossip-based decentralized training baselines in generalization and efficiency.
Attained results comparable to first-order optimization methods in large-scale distributed environments.
Eliminated the primary scalability bottleneck in decentralized training by utilizing seed-reconstructible updates.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce SeedFlood, a decentralized training method using seed-based zeroth-order updates to scale LLM training with zero overhead.

title: High-Fidelity Teleportation of Continuous-Variable Quantum States Via Non-Ideal Qutrit Entangled Resources
summary: Achieving near-unity fidelity in conventional continuous-variable quantum teleportation schemes based on two-mode squeezed vacuum states is fundamentally unattainable. To overcome this limitation, alternative approaches utilizing ensembles of two-dimensional entangled qubits have been proposed. In this work, we investigate continuous-variable quantum teleportation employing entangled qutrit resources under realistic noise effects. The results demonstrate that the proposed scheme performs well in both ideal and noisy conditions, enabling high-fidelity teleportation with a reasonable success probability.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a novel **continuous-variable quantum teleportation** architecture that replaces standard two-mode squeezed vacuum states with **entangled qutrit resources**.
- Addresses the fundamental fidelity limitations of conventional schemes by utilizing higher-dimensional **non-ideal entangled states** to improve state reconstruction.
- Analyzes the system's performance under **realistic noise effects**, demonstrating significant robustness compared to traditional qubit-based or Gaussian methods.
- Focuses on achieving **near-unity fidelity**, which is typically unattainable in standard continuous-variable implementations.
key_results: Attained higher teleportation fidelity than standard two-mode squeezed vacuum state schemes.
Successfully demonstrated high-fidelity transmission under non-ideal, noisy environmental conditions.
Established a viable success probability for teleportation using three-dimensional qutrit systems.
Validated that qutrit-based entanglement offers superior error resilience over qubit-based systems.
Provided a theoretical framework for scaling continuous-variable states via discrete-variable resources.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that entangled qutrit resources enable high-fidelity continuous-variable quantum teleportation even under realistic noise conditions.

title: Improving Sampling for Masked Diffusion Models via Information Gain
summary: Masked Diffusion Models (MDMs) offer greater flexibility in decoding order than autoregressive models but require careful planning to achieve high-quality generation. Existing samplers typically adopt greedy heuristics, prioritizing positions with the highest local certainty to decode at each step. Through failure case analysis, we identify a fundamental limitation of this approach: it neglects the downstream impact of current decoding choices on subsequent steps and fails to minimize cumulative uncertainty. In particular, these methods do not fully exploit the non-causal nature of MDMs, which enables evaluating how a decoding decision reshapes token probabilities/uncertainty across all remaining masked positions. To bridge this gap, we propose the Info-Gain Sampler, a principled decoding framework that balances immediate uncertainty with information gain over future masked tokens. Extensive evaluations across diverse architectures and tasks (reasoning, coding, creative writing, and image generation) demonstrate that Info-Gain Sampler consistently outperforms existing samplers for MDMs. For instance, it achieves a 3.6% improvement in average accuracy on reasoning tasks and a 63.1% win-rate in creative writing. Notably, on reasoning tasks it reduces cumulative uncertainty from 78.4 to 48.6, outperforming the best baseline by a large margin. The code will be available at https://github.com/yks23/Information-Gain-Sampler.

processed.summary:
- Introduces the **Info-Gain Sampler**, a decoding framework for **Masked Diffusion Models (MDMs)** that replaces greedy local heuristics with a global uncertainty minimization strategy.
- Leverages the **non-causal nature** of MDMs to evaluate how current token choices affect the probability distribution and entropy of all remaining masked positions.
- Bridges the gap between immediate local certainty and **long-term information gain**, optimizing the generation path to reduce cumulative error propagation.
- Validates performance across diverse modalities including **reasoning, coding, and image generation**, proving the sampler's versatility across different model architectures.
key_results: Achieved a 3.6% average accuracy increase on reasoning-intensive tasks.
Secured a 63.1% win-rate against existing samplers in creative writing benchmarks.
Reduced cumulative uncertainty scores from 78.4 to 48.6 on reasoning datasets.
Significantly outperformed the best baseline samplers in both text and image generation domains.
Demonstrated that considering downstream impact of tokens leads to higher quality global generation than greedy decoding.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce the Info-Gain Sampler to improve Masked Diffusion Model generation by minimizing cumulative uncertainty across decoding steps.

title: Can AI Lower the Barrier to Cybersecurity? A Human-Centered Mixed-Methods Study of Novice CTF Learning
summary:
Capture-the-Flag (CTF) competitions serve as gateways into offensive cybersecurity, yet they often present steep barriers for novices due to complex toolchains and opaque workflows. Recently, agentic AI frameworks for cybersecurity promise to lower these barriers by automating and coordinating penetration testing tasks. However, their role in shaping novice learning remains underexplored.
  We present a human-centered, mixed-methods case study examining how agentic AI frameworks -- here Cybersecurity AI (CAI) -- mediates novice entry into CTF-based penetration testing. An undergraduate student without prior hacking experience attempted to approach performance benchmarks from a national cybersecurity challenge using CAI. Quantitative performance metrics were complemented by structured reflective analysis of learning progression and AI interaction patterns.
  Our thematic analysis suggest that agentic AI reduces initial entry barriers by providing overview, structure and guidance, thereby lowering the cognitive workload during early engagement. Quantitatively, the observed extensive exploration of strategies and low per-strategy execution time potetially facilitatates cybersecurity training on meta, i.e. strategic levels. At the same time, AI-assisted cybersecurity education introduces new challenges related to trust, dependency, and responsible use. We discuss implications for human-centered AI-supported cybersecurity education and outline open questions for future research.

processed.summary:
* Evaluates a **Cybersecurity AI (CAI)** agentic framework designed to automate and coordinate penetration testing tasks for novice users.
* Employs a **human-centered mixed-methods** approach to analyze how AI mediates entry into offensive cybersecurity Capture-the-Flag (CTF) environments.
* Documents a shift from low-level tool execution to **strategic meta-analysis**, effectively reducing cognitive workload during early engagement.
* Examines the trade-offs between **rapid strategy exploration** and the emerging risks of user trust and dependency on automated agents.
key_results: Agentic AI significantly lowers the initial entry barrier for novices in offensive cybersecurity challenges.
The framework provides overview and structure that reduces cognitive workload for complex toolchains.
Quantitative data shows high strategy exploration rates with low per-strategy execution time.
Novice learning shifts from tactical tool-chaining to higher-level strategic decision-making.
Identifies critical risks regarding over-reliance and the erosion of fundamental skill acquisition.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers show that agentic AI frameworks reduce cognitive load for novice hackers by automating complex cybersecurity workflows.

title: Click it or Leave it: Detecting and Spoiling Clickbait with Informativeness Measures and Large Language Models
summary: Clickbait headlines degrade the quality of online information and undermine user trust. We present a hybrid approach to clickbait detection that combines transformer-based text embeddings with linguistically motivated informativeness features. Using natural language processing techniques, we evaluate classical vectorizers, word embedding baselines, and large language model embeddings paired with tree-based classifiers. Our best-performing model, XGBoost over embeddings augmented with 15 explicit features, achieves an F1-score of 91\%, outperforming TF-IDF, Word2Vec, GloVe, LLM prompt based classification, and feature-only baselines. The proposed feature set enhances interpretability by highlighting salient linguistic cues such as second-person pronouns, superlatives, numerals, and attention-oriented punctuation, enabling transparent and well-calibrated clickbait predictions. We release code and trained models to support reproducible research.

processed.summary:
* Proposed a **hybrid architecture** that integrates **transformer-based text embeddings** with 15 handcrafted **linguistically motivated features** to identify deceptive headlines.
* Evaluated the performance of **XGBoost** as a top-layer classifier, comparing it against classical vectorizers, **Word2Vec**, and zero-shot **LLM prompting** strategies.
* Focused on **informativeness measures** like superlatives and attention-oriented punctuation to improve the **interpretability** and calibration of the detection system.
* Demonstrated that augmenting **Large Language Model** embeddings with explicit linguistic cues significantly boosts **classification accuracy** over baseline neural approaches.
key_results: Achieved a peak F1-score of 91% using XGBoost on feature-augmented embeddings.
Outperformed standard LLM prompt-based classification and traditional TF-IDF baselines.
Identified second-person pronouns and numerals as high-signal features for clickbait detection.
Validated that 15 explicit features provide better model calibration than embeddings alone.
Released open-source code and pre-trained models to ensure research reproducibility.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that XGBoost paired with LLM embeddings and linguistic features achieves a 91% F1-score in clickbait detection.

title: A Deep Surrogate Model for Robust and Generalizable Long-Term Blast Wave Prediction
summary: Accurately modeling the spatio-temporal dynamics of blast wave propagation remains a longstanding challenge due to its highly nonlinear behavior, sharp gradients, and burdensome computational cost. While machine learning-based surrogate models offer fast inference as a promising alternative, they suffer from degraded accuracy, particularly evaluated on complex urban layouts or out-of-distribution scenarios. Moreover, autoregressive prediction strategies in such models are prone to error accumulation over long forecasting horizons, limiting their robustness for extended-time simulations. To address these limitations, we propose RGD-Blast, a robust and generalizable deep surrogate model for high-fidelity, long-term blast wave forecasting. RGD-Blast incorporates a multi-scale module to capture both global flow patterns and local boundary interactions, effectively mitigating error accumulation during autoregressive prediction. We introduce a dynamic-static feature coupling mechanism that fuses time-varying pressure fields with static source and layout features, thereby enhancing out-of-distribution generalization. Experiments demonstrate that RGD-Blast achieves a two-order-of-magnitude speedup over traditional numerical methods while maintaining comparable accuracy. In generalization tests on unseen building layouts, the model achieves an average RMSE below 0.01 and an R2 exceeding 0.89 over 280 consecutive time steps. Additional evaluations under varying blast source locations and explosive charge weights further validate its generalization, substantially advancing the state of the art in long-term blast wave modeling.

processed.summary:
*   Introduces **RGD-Blast**, a specialized deep surrogate model for high-fidelity, long-term **blast wave forecasting** in complex urban environments.
*   Features a **multi-scale module** designed to capture both global flow dynamics and local boundary interactions, significantly mitigating **autoregressive error accumulation**.
*   Employs a **dynamic-static feature coupling** mechanism that integrates time-varying pressure fields with static geometric features to improve **OOD (out-of-distribution) generalization**.
*   Provides a massive computational advantage by delivering a **two-order-of-magnitude speedup** compared to traditional physics-based numerical solvers.
key_results: Achieved a 100x speedup over traditional numerical methods for blast wave simulation.
Maintained high precision with an average RMSE below 0.01 across 280 time steps.
Demonstrated strong generalization on unseen building layouts with R2 scores exceeding 0.89.
Successfully reduced error drift in long-term forecasting through multi-scale feature extraction.
Validated robustness against variations in explosive charge weights and source locations.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce RGD-Blast, a multi-scale surrogate model that achieves 100x faster blast wave simulations with superior generalization.

title: Unifying Formal Explanations: A Complexity-Theoretic Perspective
summary: Previous work has explored the computational complexity of deriving two fundamental types of explanations for ML model predictions: (1) *sufficient reasons*, which are subsets of input features that, when fixed, determine a prediction, and (2) *contrastive reasons*, which are subsets of input features that, when modified, alter a prediction. Prior studies have examined these explanations in different contexts, such as non-probabilistic versus probabilistic frameworks and local versus global settings. In this study, we introduce a unified framework for analyzing these explanations, demonstrating that they can all be characterized through the minimization of a unified probabilistic value function. We then prove that the complexity of these computations is influenced by three key properties of the value function: (1) *monotonicity*, (2) *submodularity*, and (3) *supermodularity* - which are three fundamental properties in *combinatorial optimization*. Our findings uncover some counterintuitive results regarding the nature of these properties within the explanation settings examined. For instance, although the *local* value functions do not exhibit monotonicity or submodularity/supermodularity whatsoever, we demonstrate that the *global* value functions do possess these properties. This distinction enables us to prove a series of novel polynomial-time results for computing various explanations with provable guarantees in the global explainability setting, across a range of ML models that span the interpretability spectrum, such as neural networks, decision trees, and tree ensembles. In contrast, we show that even highly simplified versions of these explanations become NP-hard to compute in the corresponding local explainability setting.

processed.summary:
* Proposes a **unified framework** for formal explanations by reducing both sufficient and contrastive reasons to the minimization of a probabilistic value function.
* Identifies that **global explainability** possesses properties of **monotonicity and submodularity**, allowing for polynomial-time computation with provable guarantees.
* Demonstrates that **local explainability** remains **NP-hard** even in simplified scenarios because it lacks the core combinatorial properties found in global settings.
* Provides a bridge between **combinatorial optimization** and interpretability for various models, including neural networks and tree ensembles.
key_results: Development of a unified value function characterizing both sufficient and contrastive explanations.
Discovery that global value functions exhibit monotonicity, submodularity, and supermodularity.
Proof of polynomial-time results for computing global explanations across diverse ML architectures.
Validation that local value functions fail to exhibit these optimization properties, confirming NP-hardness.
Establishment of formal guarantees for interpretability settings in neural networks and decision trees.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers unify formal ML explanations using combinatorial optimization to achieve polynomial-time results for global model interpretability.

title: FENCE: A Financial and Multimodal Jailbreak Detection Dataset
summary: Jailbreaking poses a significant risk to the deployment of Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision Language Models (VLMs). VLMs are particularly vulnerable because they process both text and images, creating broader attack surfaces. However, available resources for jailbreak detection are scarce, particularly in finance. To address this gap, we present FENCE, a bilingual (Korean-English) multimodal dataset for training and evaluating jailbreak detectors in financial applications. FENCE emphasizes domain realism through finance-relevant queries paired with image-grounded threats. Experiments with commercial and open-source VLMs reveal consistent vulnerabilities, with GPT-4o showing measurable attack success rates and open-source models displaying greater exposure. A baseline detector trained on FENCE achieves 99 percent in-distribution accuracy and maintains strong performance on external benchmarks, underscoring the dataset's robustness for training reliable detection models. FENCE provides a focused resource for advancing multimodal jailbreak detection in finance and for supporting safer, more reliable AI systems in sensitive domains. Warning: This paper includes example data that may be offensive.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **FENCE**, a bilingual (Korean-English) **multimodal dataset** designed specifically for training and evaluating jailbreak detectors within financial contexts.
- Addresses the unique vulnerabilities of **Vision Language Models (VLMs)** by pairing domain-specific financial queries with **image-grounded threats** to expand known attack surfaces.
- Facilitates the development of **robust safety filters** for sensitive applications, demonstrating that specialized training data can yield high-accuracy detection models.
key_results: Development of the first bilingual multimodal jailbreak dataset focused on the financial domain.
Discovery of measurable attack success rates on GPT-4o using image-grounded financial threats.
Observation that open-source VLMs exhibit significantly higher exposure to jailbreak attacks than proprietary models.
Baseline detector trained on FENCE achieved 99 percent in-distribution accuracy.
Demonstrated strong generalization of the FENCE-trained detector on external, non-financial benchmarks.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce FENCE, a bilingual multimodal dataset that significantly improves jailbreak detection accuracy for financial Vision Language Models.

title: The Statistical Signature of LLMs
summary: Large language models generate text through probabilistic sampling from high-dimensional distributions, yet how this process reshapes the structural statistical organization of language remains incompletely characterized. Here we show that lossless compression provides a simple, model-agnostic measure of statistical regularity that differentiates generative regimes directly from surface text. We analyze compression behavior across three progressively more complex information ecosystems: controlled human-LLM continuations, generative mediation of a knowledge infrastructure (Wikipedia vs. Grokipedia), and fully synthetic social interaction environments (Moltbook vs. Reddit). Across settings, compression reveals a persistent structural signature of probabilistic generation. In controlled and mediated contexts, LLM-produced language exhibits higher structural regularity and compressibility than human-written text, consistent with a concentration of output within highly recurrent statistical patterns. However, this signature shows scale dependence: in fragmented interaction environments the separation attenuates, suggesting a fundamental limit to surface-level distinguishability at small scales. This compressibility-based separation emerges consistently across models, tasks, and domains and can be observed directly from surface text without relying on model internals or semantic evaluation. Overall, our findings introduce a simple and robust framework for quantifying how generative systems reshape textual production, offering a structural perspective on the evolving complexity of communication.

processed.summary:
- Utilizes **lossless compression** as a model-agnostic metric to quantify the statistical regularity and structural organization of LLM-generated text.
- Demonstrates that AI-produced language is significantly more **compressible** than human text, revealing a concentration of output within highly recurrent statistical patterns.
- Identifies a **scale-dependent signature** where the statistical distinction between human and synthetic text attenuates in fragmented, small-scale interaction environments.
- Establishes a robust framework for **AI evaluation** and detection based purely on surface-level text statistics without requiring access to model weights or internal states.
key_results: LLM-generated text consistently exhibits higher structural regularity than human-written text across multiple domains.
Lossless compression serves as an effective, model-agnostic proxy for identifying the probabilistic sampling fingerprint of generative models.
AI-mediated knowledge infrastructures (e.g., Grokipedia) show significantly higher predictability than human-curated counterparts.
The gap in compressibility between humans and AI narrows in short, fragmented social media-style interactions, suggesting limits to detection.
The identified statistical signature persists across different model architectures, training objectives, and complexity levels.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that LLM-generated text possesses a distinct statistical signature of high compressibility compared to human-authored content.

title: Rethinking Beam Management: Generalization Limits Under Hardware Heterogeneity
summary: Hardware heterogeneity across diverse user devices poses new challenges for beam-based communication in 5G and beyond. This heterogeneity limits the applicability of machine learning (ML)-based algorithms. This article highlights the critical need to treat hardware heterogeneity as a first-class design concern in ML-aided beam management. We analyze key failure modes in the presence of heterogeneity and present case studies demonstrating their performance impact. Finally, we discuss potential strategies to improve generalization in beam management.

processed.summary:
- The paper investigates how **hardware heterogeneity** across 5G/6G user devices creates significant performance gaps in **ML-aided beam management**.
- It characterizes specific **failure modes** where models trained on homogeneous datasets fail to generalize to diverse device antenna architectures.
- The research advocates for an architectural shift that treats **hardware-specific variance** as a primary design constraint rather than a noise factor.
- Impact analysis shows that without addressing these limits, **out-of-distribution (OOD)** hardware configurations lead to critical communication link failures.
key_results: Quantification of performance degradation when ML beam models encounter unseen hardware configurations.
Identification of signal pattern mismatches caused by device-specific RF front-ends.
Demonstration that standard ML training lacks the robustness required for multi-vendor device ecosystems.
Analysis of case studies showing the impact of heterogeneity on beam prediction accuracy.
Proposed framework for incorporating hardware-aware features into beam management algorithms.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that hardware heterogeneity significantly degrades ML-aided beam management performance, necessitating new hardware-aware generalization strategies.

title: Stable Long-Horizon Spatiotemporal Prediction on Meshes Using Latent Multiscale Recurrent Graph Neural Networks
summary: Accurate long-horizon prediction of spatiotemporal fields on complex geometries is a fundamental challenge in scientific machine learning, with applications such as additive manufacturing where temperature histories govern defect formation and mechanical properties. High-fidelity simulations are accurate but computationally costly, and despite recent advances, machine learning methods remain challenged by long-horizon temperature and gradient prediction. We propose a deep learning framework for predicting full temperature histories directly on meshes, conditioned on geometry and process parameters, while maintaining stability over thousands of time steps and generalizing across heterogeneous geometries. The framework adopts a temporal multiscale architecture composed of two coupled models operating at complementary time scales. Both models rely on a latent recurrent graph neural network to capture spatiotemporal dynamics on meshes, while a variational graph autoencoder provides a compact latent representation that reduces memory usage and improves training stability. Experiments on simulated powder bed fusion data demonstrate accurate and temporally stable long-horizon predictions across diverse geometries, outperforming existing baseline. Although evaluated in two dimensions, the framework is general and extensible to physics-driven systems with multiscale dynamics and to three-dimensional geometries.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a **temporal multiscale architecture** utilizing two coupled models to handle dynamics across complementary time scales.
- Employs **Latent Recurrent Graph Neural Networks (GNNs)** to model complex spatiotemporal dynamics directly on unstructured meshes.
- Integrates a **Variational Graph Autoencoder (VGAE)** to create a compressed latent representation, significantly reducing memory overhead and improving training stability.
- Enables **stable long-horizon predictions** over thousands of steps, generalizing across heterogeneous geometries in physics-driven systems.

title: Detecting Contextual Hallucinations in LLMs with Frequency-Aware Attention
summary: Hallucination detection is critical for ensuring the reliability of large language models (LLMs) in context-based generation. Prior work has explored intrinsic signals available during generation, among which attention offers a direct view of grounding behavior. However, existing approaches typically rely on coarse summaries that fail to capture fine-grained instabilities in attention. Inspired by signal processing, we introduce a frequency-aware perspective on attention by analyzing its variation during generation. We model attention distributions as discrete signals and extract high-frequency components that reflect rapid local changes in attention. Our analysis reveals that hallucinated tokens are associated with high-frequency attention energy, reflecting fragmented and unstable grounding behavior. Based on this insight, we develop a lightweight hallucination detector using high-frequency attention features. Experiments on the RAGTruth and HalluRAG benchmarks show that our approach achieves performance gains over verification-based, internal-representation-based, and attention-based methods across models and tasks.

• Introduces a **frequency-aware perspective** on attention mechanisms by modeling attention distributions as discrete signals to detect contextual hallucinations.
• Extracts **high-frequency components** from attention maps to identify rapid local changes that signal unstable or fragmented grounding behavior.
• Develops a **lightweight detector** based on these spectral features, offering a more granular diagnostic than traditional coarse attention summaries.
• Enhances reliability in **RAG environments** by providing a signal-based method to verify token-level generation integrity.
key_results: Discovered that hallucinated tokens correlate strongly with high-frequency attention energy.
Proposed a novel signal processing framework for interpreting LLM internal attention states.
Outperformed verification-based and representation-based baselines on the RAGTruth benchmark.
Demonstrated cross-model generalizability of high-frequency features for hallucination detection.
Achieved significant performance gains on the HalluRAG dataset compared to state-of-the-art methods.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers utilize frequency-aware attention analysis to detect LLM hallucinations by identifying fragmented and unstable grounding signals.

title: Toward Automated Virtual Electronic Control Unit (ECU) Twins for Shift-Left Automotive Software Testing
summary: Automotive software increasingly outpaces hardware availability, forcing late integration and expensive hardware-in-the-loop (HiL) bottlenecks. The InnoRegioChallenge project investigated whether a virtual test and integration environment can reproduce electronic control unit (ECU) behavior early enough to run real software binaries before physical hardware exists. We report a prototype that generates instruction-accurate processor models in SystemC/TLM~2.0 using an agentic, feedback-driven workflow coupled to a reference simulator via the GNU Debugger (GDB). The results indicate that the most critical technical risk -- CPU behavioral fidelity -- can be reduced through automated differential testing and iterative model correction. We summarize the architecture, the agentic modeling loop, and project outcomes, and we extrapolate plausible technical details consistent with the reported qualitative findings. While cloud-scale deployment and full toolchain integration remain future work, the prototype demonstrates a viable shift-left path for virtual ECU twins, enabling reproducible tests, non-intrusive tracing, and fault-injection campaigns aligned with safety standards.

processed.summary:
The project proposes an **agentic, feedback-driven workflow** to automate the generation of instruction-accurate processor models for virtual Electronic Control Unit (ECU) twins.
Utilizes **SystemC/TLM 2.0** architecture integrated with the **GNU Debugger (GDB)** to perform automated differential testing against reference simulators.
Focuses on a **shift-left testing strategy**, allowing developers to run real software binaries before physical hardware is available, thereby bypassing Hardware-in-the-Loop (HiL) bottlenecks.

Significant reduction in CPU behavioral fidelity risks through automated differential testing.
Successful execution of real software binaries on virtual instruction-accurate models.

Demonstration of a viable path for cloud-scale deployment of virtual hardware twins.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: The InnoRegioChallenge project uses an agentic workflow to automate virtual ECU twins, enabling early, high-fidelity automotive software testing.

title: Advection-Diffusion on Graphs: A Bakry-Emery Laplacian for Spectral Graph Neural Networks
summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) often struggle to propagate information across long distances due to oversmoothing and oversquashing. Existing remedies such as graph transformers or rewiring typically incur high computational cost or require altering the graph structure. We introduce a Bakry-Emery graph Laplacian that integrates diffusion and advection through a learnable node-wise potential, inducing task-dependent propagation dynamics without modifying topology. This operator has a well-behaved spectral decomposition and acts as a drop-in replacement for standard Laplacians in spectral GNNs. Building on this insight, we develop mu-ChebNet, a spectral architecture that jointly learns the potential and Chebyshev filters, effectively bridging message-passing adaptivity and spectral efficiency. Our theoretical analysis shows how the potential modulates the spectrum, enabling control of key graph properties. Empirically, mu-ChebNet delivers consistent gains on synthetic long-range reasoning tasks, as well as real-world benchmarks, while offering an interpretable routing field that reveals how information flows through the graph. This establishes the Bakry-Emery Laplacian as a principled and efficient foundation for adaptive spectral graph learning.

* Introduces the **Bakry-Emery Laplacian**, a novel operator that integrates diffusion and advection through a learnable node-wise potential to mitigate **oversmoothing** and **oversquashing**.
* Proposes **mu-ChebNet**, a spectral GNN architecture that jointly optimizes task-dependent potentials and Chebyshev filters for efficient, adaptive information propagation.
* Enables **long-range reasoning** without the high computational costs of graph transformers or the topological changes required by graph rewiring methods.
* Provides a well-behaved **spectral decomposition**, making the operator a drop-in replacement for standard Laplacians in existing spectral graph neural network frameworks.
key_results: Successfully integrates advection-diffusion dynamics into spectral GNNs via a learnable potential.
Mu-ChebNet effectively bridges the gap between message-passing adaptivity and spectral efficiency.
Theoretical proof that node-wise potentials modulate the graph spectrum to control information flow.
Significant performance gains on synthetic long-range reasoning tasks compared to traditional GNNs.
Generates interpretable routing fields that visualize the direction and intensity of information flow.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce the Bakry-Emery Laplacian to improve long-range graph reasoning through learnable, task-dependent advection-diffusion dynamics.

title: Agentic Adversarial QA for Improving Domain-Specific LLMs
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs), despite extensive pretraining on broad internet corpora, often struggle to adapt effectively to specialized domains. There is growing interest in fine-tuning these models for such domains; however, progress is constrained by the scarcity and limited coverage of high-quality, task-relevant data. To address this, synthetic data generation methods such as paraphrasing or knowledge extraction are commonly applied. Although these approaches excel at factual recall and conceptual knowledge, they suffer from two critical shortcomings: (i) they provide minimal support for interpretive reasoning capabilities in these specialized domains, and (ii) they often produce synthetic corpora that are excessively large and redundant, resulting in poor sample efficiency. To overcome these gaps, we propose an adversarial question-generation framework that produces a compact set of semantically challenging questions. These questions are constructed by comparing the outputs of the model to be adapted and a robust expert model grounded in reference documents, using an iterative, feedback-driven process designed to reveal and address comprehension gaps. Evaluation on specialized subsets of the LegalBench corpus demonstrates that our method achieves greater accuracy with substantially fewer synthetic samples.

processed.summary:
- Proposes an **agentic adversarial framework** designed to generate high-quality synthetic data for domain-specific fine-tuning.
- Utilizes an **iterative feedback loop** that identifies reasoning gaps by comparing a target model's outputs against a grounded **expert model**.
- Prioritizes **interpretive reasoning** and semantic difficulty over simple factual recall to address the limitations of traditional synthetic corpora.
- Increases **sample efficiency** by creating a compact dataset of challenging questions that target specific comprehension weaknesses.
key_results: Achieved higher accuracy on specialized LegalBench subsets compared to traditional synthetic methods.
Significantly reduced the number of synthetic samples required for effective domain adaptation.
Improved model performance in complex interpretive reasoning tasks within the legal domain.
Validated the effectiveness of using a grounded expert model to generate semantically challenging questions.
Demonstrated a successful iterative process for identifying and closing model knowledge gaps.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce an adversarial agentic framework that boosts domain-specific LLM accuracy using fewer, high-quality synthetic reasoning samples.

title: Cavity Solitons as a Nonlinear Substrate for Photonic Neuromorphic Computing
summary: Reservoir computing leverages nonlinear dynamics of physical systems to process temporal information with minimal training cost. Here, we demonstrate that cavity solitons sustained in a fiber optical cavity provide an optical platform for photonic reservoir computing. Our methodology exploits the use of a phase-modulated drive laser to encode the input, while the reservoir states are accessed through frequency-resolved readout. Numerical simulations indicate that the emission of Kelly waves enriches the dynamics and enhances performance for machine learning tasks. We evaluate the performance of the cavity-soliton reservoir computer on several standard benchmark tasks.

* Employs a **phase-modulated drive laser** for input encoding and **frequency-resolved readout** to access high-dimensional reservoir states.
* Demonstrates that the emission of **Kelly waves** creates complex nonlinear dynamics that significantly improve machine learning performance and computational richness.

Successful implementation of a phase-modulation scheme for high-speed input encoding.
Identification of frequency-resolved readout as an efficient method for reservoir state extraction.

title: TempoNet: Slack-Quantized Transformer-Guided Reinforcement Scheduler for Adaptive Deadline-Centric Real-Time Dispatchs
summary: Real-time schedulers must reason about tight deadlines under strict compute budgets. We present TempoNet, a reinforcement learning scheduler that pairs a permutation-invariant Transformer with a deep Q-approximation. An Urgency Tokenizer discretizes temporal slack into learnable embeddings, stabilizing value learning and capturing deadline proximity. A latency-aware sparse attention stack with blockwise top-k selection and locality-sensitive chunking enables global reasoning over unordered task sets with near-linear scaling and sub-millisecond inference. A multicore mapping layer converts contextualized Q-scores into processor assignments through masked-greedy selection or differentiable matching. Extensive evaluations on industrial mixed-criticality traces and large multiprocessor settings show consistent gains in deadline fulfillment over analytic schedulers and neural baselines, together with improved optimization stability. Diagnostics include sensitivity analyses for slack quantization, attention-driven policy interpretation, hardware-in-the-loop and kernel micro-benchmarks, and robustness under stress with simple runtime mitigations; we also report sample-efficiency benefits from behavioral-cloning pretraining and compatibility with an actor-critic variant without altering the inference pipeline. These results establish a practical framework for Transformer-based decision making in high-throughput real-time scheduling.

• Features a novel **Urgency Tokenizer** that discretizes temporal slack into learnable embeddings to stabilize value learning and capture deadline proximity.
• Utilizes a **latency-aware sparse attention** stack with blockwise top-k selection, enabling sub-millisecond inference and near-linear scaling for large task sets.
• Employs a **multicore mapping layer** for processor assignment, supported by **behavioral-cloning pretraining** to enhance sample efficiency and optimization stability.

title: A contour for the entanglement negativity of bosonic Gaussian states
summary: We construct a contour function for the logarithmic negativity and the logarithm of the moments of the partial transpose of the reduced density matrix for multimode bosonic Gaussian states of a free lattice model. In one spatial dimension, numerical results are obtained for harmonic chains either in the ground state or at finite temperature, by considering, respectively, either a subsystem made by two adjacent or disjoint blocks on the line or a bipartition of the circle. The contour function of the logarithmic negativity diverges only at the entangling points, while the contour function for the logarithm of the moments of the partial transpose is divergent also at the boundary of the bipartite subsystem, as functions of the position. In a two-dimensional conformal field theory, analytic expressions that describe these divergencies are discussed. In one spatial dimension, we explore the partial derivative of the logarithmic negativity of two adjacent intervals with respect to the logarithm of the harmonic ratio of their lengths while their ratio and the other parameters are kept fixed. Considering the ground state of the harmonic chain on the line and in the massive regime, we report numerical results showing that this quantity displays a monotonically decreasing behaviour.

- It provides **numerical results** for harmonic chains in one dimension, exploring both ground states and finite-temperature regimes for disjoint and adjacent subsystems.
- The research utilizes **conformal field theory (CFT)** to derive analytical expressions for divergences observed at entangling points and subsystem boundaries.
- The authors analyze the **partial derivative** of logarithmic negativity in massive regimes, demonstrating monotonically decreasing behavior relative to harmonic ratios.

Analytical description of divergences in 2D conformal field theory frameworks.

title: Fair Orientations: Proportionality and Equitability
summary: We study the fair allocation of indivisible items under relevance constraints, where each agent has a set of relevant items and can only receive items that are relevant to them. While the relevance constraint has been studied in recent years, existing work has largely focused on envy-freeness. Our work extends this study to other key fairness criteria -- such as proportionality, equitability, and their relaxations -- in settings where the items may be goods, chores, or a mixture of both. We complement the literature by presenting a picture of the existence and computational complexity of the considered criteria.

processed.summary:
• Investigates the **fair allocation** of indivisible items including goods, chores, and mixed sets under **relevance constraints** where agents only receive items they find pertinent.
 • Analyzes the existence and **computational complexity** of various fairness metrics, specifically focusing on **proportionality (Prop)** and **equitability (EQ)**.
 • Establishes a theoretical framework for **fair orientations**, providing a comprehensive map of feasibility for relaxed criteria such as Prop1 and EQ1 in multi-agent environments.

title: Neurosymbolic Language Reasoning as Satisfiability Modulo Theory
summary: Natural language understanding requires interleaving textual and logical reasoning, yet large language models often fail to perform such reasoning reliably. Existing neurosymbolic systems combine LLMs with solvers but remain limited to fully formalizable tasks such as math or program synthesis, leaving natural documents with only partial logical structure unaddressed. We introduce Logitext, a neurosymbolic language that represents documents as natural language text constraints (NLTCs), making partial logical structure explicit. We develop an algorithm that integrates LLM-based constraint evaluation with satisfiability modulo theory (SMT) solving, enabling joint textual-logical reasoning. Experiments on a new content moderation benchmark, together with LegalBench and Super-Natural Instructions, show that Logitext improves both accuracy and coverage. This work is the first that treats LLM-based reasoning as an SMT theory, extending neurosymbolic methods beyond fully formalizable domains.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **Logitext**, a neurosymbolic framework that represents documents as **Natural Language Text Constraints (NLTCs)** to capture partial logical structures.
- Developed an algorithm that treats **LLM-based constraint evaluation as an SMT theory**, allowing formal solvers to interleave textual interpretation with symbolic logic.
- Extends the reach of neurosymbolic methods beyond rigid domains like math or code to **semi-structured natural language** tasks such as legal analysis.
key_results: Defined Natural Language Text Constraints (NLTCs) for modeling semi-structured documents
First implementation of LLM reasoning integrated as a theory within a Satisfiability Modulo Theory (SMT) solver
Achieved significant accuracy gains on the LegalBench and Super-Natural Instructions datasets

Demonstrated improved coverage and reliability compared to vanilla LLM prompting in reasoning tasks

title: Perceived Political Bias in LLMs Reduces Persuasive Abilities
summary: Conversational AI has been proposed as a scalable way to correct public misconceptions and spread misinformation. Yet its effectiveness may depend on perceptions of its political neutrality. As LLMs enter partisan conflict, elites increasingly portray them as ideologically aligned. We test whether these credibility attacks reduce LLM-based persuasion. In a preregistered U.S. survey experiment (N=2144), participants completed a three-round conversation with ChatGPT about a personally held economic policy misconception. Compared to a neutral control, a short message indicating that the LLM was biased against the respondent's party attenuated persuasion by 28%. Transcript analysis indicates that the warnings alter the interaction: respondents push back more and engage less receptively. These findings suggest that the persuasive impact of conversational AI is politically contingent, constrained by perceptions of partisan alignment.

processed.summary:
- The study evaluates the **persuasive efficacy** of conversational AI when users are primed with **partisan credibility attacks**.
- Using a **multi-round dialogue** structure (three rounds) with ChatGPT, researchers measured belief shifts regarding economic policy misconceptions.
- Findings indicate that **perceived ideological bias** significantly undermines the model's ability to correct misinformation by 28%.
- Behavioral analysis of transcripts reveals that negative priming shifts user interactions from receptive learning to **adversarial push-back**.
key_results: Priming users about potential political bias reduced the LLM's persuasive impact by 28%.
Perceived bias leads to a significant decrease in user receptivity during conversational rounds.
Credibility attacks by elite figures can effectively 'poison the well' for AI-led information campaigns.

title: Comparative Assessment of Multimodal Earth Observation Data for Soil Moisture Estimation
summary: Accurate soil moisture (SM) estimation is critical for precision agriculture, water resources management and climate monitoring. Yet, existing satellite SM products are too coarse (>1km) for farm-level applications. We present a high-resolution (10m) SM estimation framework for vegetated areas across Europe, combining Sentinel-1 SAR, Sentinel-2 optical imagery and ERA-5 reanalysis data through machine learning. Using 113 International Soil Moisture Network (ISMN) stations spanning diverse vegetated areas, we compare modality combinations with temporal parameterizations, using spatial cross-validation, to ensure geographic generalization. We also evaluate whether foundation model embeddings from IBM-NASA's Prithvi model improve upon traditional hand-crafted spectral features. Results demonstrate that hybrid temporal matching - Sentinel-2 current-day acquisitions with Sentinel-1 descending orbit - achieves R^2=0.514, with 10-day ERA5 lookback window improving performance to R^2=0.518. Foundation model (Prithvi) embeddings provide negligible improvement over hand-crafted features (R^2=0.515 vs. 0.514), indicating traditional feature engineering remains highly competitive for sparse-data regression tasks. Our findings suggest that domain-specific spectral indices combined with tree-based ensemble methods offer a practical and computationally efficient solution for operational pan-European field-scale soil moisture monitoring.

- Developed a **multimodal machine learning framework** for 10m high-resolution soil moisture estimation using Sentinel-1 SAR, Sentinel-2 optical imagery, and ERA-5 data.
- Evaluated the performance of **foundation model embeddings** from IBM-NASA's Prithvi, finding they offer negligible improvement over **hand-crafted spectral indices**.
- Implemented **spatial cross-validation** across 113 International Soil Moisture Network (ISMN) stations to ensure geographic generalization across diverse European landscapes.
- Demonstrated that **tree-based ensemble methods** remain highly competitive and computationally efficient for sparse-data environmental regression tasks.

Foundation model (Prithvi) embeddings achieved R^2=0.515 compared to R^2=0.514 for traditional feature engineering.
Successfully scaled soil moisture estimation from >1km coarse satellite products down to a 10m field-scale resolution.

processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers show that traditional feature engineering matches foundation model performance for high-resolution multimodal soil moisture regression tasks.

title: Dynamic Deception: When Pedestrians Team Up to Fool Autonomous Cars
summary: Many adversarial attacks on autonomous-driving perception models fail to cause system-level failures once deployed in a full driving stack. The main reason for such ineffectiveness is that once deployed in a system (e.g., within a simulator), attacks tend to be spatially or temporally short-lived, due to the vehicle's dynamics, hence rarely influencing the vehicle behaviour. In this paper, we address both limitations by introducing a system-level attack in which multiple dynamic elements (e.g., two pedestrians) carry adversarial patches (e.g., on cloths) and jointly amplify their effect through coordination and motion. We evaluate our attacks in the CARLA simulator using a state-of-the-art autonomous driving agent. At the system level, single-pedestrian attacks fail in all runs (out of 10), while dynamic collusion by two pedestrians induces full vehicle stops in up to 50\% of runs, with static collusion yielding no successful attack at all. These results show that system-level failures arise only when adversarial signals persist over time and are amplified through coordinated actors, exposing a gap between model-level robustness and end-to-end safety.

processed.summary:
* Proposes a **system-level adversarial attack** framework that utilizes multiple coordinated agents (pedestrians) wearing **adversarial patches** to disrupt autonomous vehicle stacks.
* Addresses the **temporal and spatial instability** of traditional adversarial attacks by ensuring signals persist through **dynamic motion coordination**.
* Evaluated within the **CARLA simulator** to demonstrate how end-to-end safety differs from individual **model-level robustness**.
* Focuses on the **perception-to-control pipeline**, showing that vehicle dynamics usually filter out non-persistent adversarial noise unless specifically amplified.
key_results: Single-pedestrian adversarial attacks failed to cause system failures in 100% of test runs.
Dynamic collusion between two pedestrians induced full vehicle stops in 50% of scenarios.
Static collusion (multiple actors without coordinated movement) yielded a 0% success rate.
Adversarial effectiveness is highly dependent on the signal's persistence over time relative to vehicle speed.
System-level failures occur only when adversarial signals are amplified through coordinated actor behavior.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that coordinated pedestrian motion with adversarial patches can successfully trigger system-level failures in autonomous driving.
